The text discusses a research paper focusing on an AI-based Intrusion Detection System (IDS) for IoT networks using Federated Learning (FL). The central issue addressed in the paper is the need for a decentralized IDS to address the challenges of centralized solutions, including data privacy violations, scalability issues, and slow detection of malicious traffic.

The text discusses research on implementing and comparing the performance of DBN (Deep Belief Network) and DNN (Deep Neural Network) based Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) in an Internet of Things (IoT) environment, using a Federated Learning (FL) approach.

Federated Learning is a distributed learning paradigm where multiple clients collaboratively train a global model without sharing raw data, enhancing data privacy and reducing communication overhead. The clients train their local models using their own data, then send updates to a central server, which aggregates the updates

The text discusses a research focusing on the efficiency of Federated Learning (FL)-based Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) for detecting zero-day attacks in Internet of Things (IoT) devices. The FL method is compared with centralized, distributed, and localized methods, and it is found that the FL-based IDS is most efficient for the detection of zero-day attacks.

The text describes a research on an intrusion detection system (IDS) using a Federated Learning (FL) model. The attack mechanism is not explicitly stated, but the system is designed to detect various types of cyber threats such as Scan, DDoS, XSS, Pwd DoS, Back Injection, Ransomware, MITM, and normal traffic.

The text discusses a research on a federated learning (FL) based Intrusion Detection System (IDS) for IoT networks. The goal is to create a multi-class FL-IDS that performs well under non-IID (non-independent and identically distributed) conditions common in IoT networks.
